message: Hello! Welcome, and thank you for joining me today. How are you doing so far?
Hi! I'm doing well, thanks for asking. Just excited to be here and learn more about the opportunity at Zephlen. How about you?

I’m doing great, thank you for asking! It’s always a pleasure to meet candidates who are eager and enthusiastic about exploring new opportunities. I’m really looking forward to our conversation today.

Before we dive in, I’d love to know—what inspired you to apply for a role at Zephlen?

Oh, you know, I’ve been following the space for a while, and Zephlen really stands out. The whole idea of moving beyond chatbots to actual autonomous agents—that’s exactly the kind of challenge I’ve been focusing on lately. It feels like the right next step for me, especially after working on similar systems at my current role. Plus, the fact that it’s based in Istanbul is a nice bonus!

That’s fantastic to hear! It sounds like your experience and passion align really well with what we’re building here. Istanbul is a vibrant city, and we’re thrilled to have it as our home base—it’s a great place to innovate and grow.

Since you mentioned your background, I’d love to explore how your experiences have shaped your approach to work. For example, tell me about a time when you had to adapt to a significant change or unexpected challenge in a project. How did you handle it, and what did you learn from the experience?

Hmm, that’s a good question. I mean, things change all the time in tech, right? So, it depends on the situation. There was this one time where the requirements shifted pretty late in the game, but we handled it somehow. I guess I just rolled with it and tried to keep things moving forward. It’s all about being flexible, you know?

Absolutely, flexibility is key in our field. I’d love to dig a little deeper into that example if you don’t mind. You mentioned the requirements shifted late in the game—what was the specific change, and how did it impact the team’s workflow or goals? For instance, did it require reprioritizing tasks, or did you have to learn something new on the fly?

Oh, right. So, we were building this supply chain agent, and halfway through, the client suddenly wanted real-time weather data integrated into the rerouting logic. That wasn’t in the original scope at all. The team had to scramble a bit—we had to figure out how to pull live API data without breaking the existing pipeline. I ended up writing some new tool-calling logic to handle the weather alerts, but it wasn’t seamless at first. There were a few hiccups with rate limits and data formatting, but we got it working eventually. It definitely taught me to expect the unexpected, I guess.

That sounds like a great example of how unpredictable projects can be, especially when clients pivot unexpectedly. It’s impressive that you were able to step in and adapt the tool-calling logic to make it work. 

I’m curious—when you encountered those hiccups, like the rate limits and data formatting issues, how did you approach troubleshooting? Did you lean on the team for support, or did you tackle it more independently?

Oh, you know, it was a mix. I started by digging into the API docs myself to see what was causing the rate limits—turns out we were hitting their free tier limits way too fast. So I had to batch the requests and add some retry logic with exponential backoff. The data formatting was trickier because the weather API returned nested JSON, and our agent expected flat key-value pairs. I wrote a quick preprocessing script to flatten it, but then realized it was breaking some of the downstream logic. Had to loop in a teammate to help debug that part. Teamwork, right?

That’s a fantastic example of teamwork and problem-solving. It’s clear you’re someone who can dive deep into the technical details while also knowing when to collaborate with others to get things done.

Shifting gears a little, I’d love to explore how you navigate situations where you have to step outside your core role to unblock a project. For example, have you ever found yourself in a position where you had to address a non-technical challenge—like negotiating with stakeholders, aligning teams, or even advocating for resources—that wasn’t strictly part of your job description? How did you approach that situation, and what was the outcome?

Hmm, non-technical challenges... yeah, those pop up. There was this one time when the product team wanted to push a new feature for the supply chain agent, but they didn’t fully grasp the technical constraints. They kept asking for things that would’ve required a complete overhaul of the orchestration layer. I could’ve just said "no" and moved on, but instead, I set up a quick sync with them to walk through the trade-offs—like how adding X would increase latency or Y would require more compute costs.

I put together a simple doc with pros and cons, and even mocked up a lightweight version of their ask to show what was feasible. They ended up scaling back the scope, and we shipped something that worked without derailing the whole project. Not sure if that counts as "negotiating," but it felt like the right way to handle it.

You handled that situation really well—it’s clear you’re someone who can bridge the gap between technical and non-technical teams, which is so valuable in collaborative environments like ours.

I’d love to explore how you approach learning and growth, especially when faced with unexpected changes. For example, think about a time when a tool, framework, or project you were deeply invested in suddenly became obsolete or was overshadowed by something new. How did you react in the moment, and what steps did you take to adapt? Did you pivot, double down, or move on entirely?

Oh man, that’s happened more times than I can count. I remember when LangChain was *the* thing everyone was using for agent workflows. We built our entire initial pipeline on it, but then we started hitting walls—like, the orchestration wasn’t deterministic enough, and debugging multi-step chains was a nightmare. At first, I was pretty frustrated. I’d spent months learning it, and now it felt like we were back to square one.

But instead of digging in my heels, I took a step back and asked, *"What’s the actual problem we’re trying to solve?"* Turns out, we needed more control over the agent’s reasoning loop. So I spent a weekend prototyping a custom orchestration engine in Python—just a simple state machine at first, but it let us define the exact flow we wanted. When I showed it to the team, they were skeptical at first, but after we ran some benchmarks and saw the task completion rate jump, it became the new standard. Sometimes you just gotta let go of the tools and focus on the outcome, you know?

That’s such a great example of resilience and adaptability. It’s not easy to let go of something you’ve invested time and energy into, but your ability to refocus on the bigger picture and prototype a solution is exactly the kind of mindset we value here.

You’ve shared some really insightful stories today, and I feel like I’ve gotten a great sense of your experience and approach. Before we wrap up, is there anything else you’d like to share about your background, your goals, or why you’re excited about the opportunity at Zephlen?

Yeah, I’ve really enjoyed this conversation—it’s been great to talk through some of these experiences. I think what excites me most about Zephlen is the chance to work on *truly* autonomous agents. At my current role, we’ve built some powerful systems, but there’s still so much untapped potential in making agents more reliable, more adaptable, and more *useful* in real-world workflows. I love the idea of pushing that boundary further, especially in a team that’s just as obsessed with the details as I am.

And honestly, the fact that Zephlen is based in Istanbul but thinking globally is a huge draw. I’ve always wanted to work somewhere that’s rooted in the local tech scene but isn’t afraid to compete on a bigger stage. Plus, I’ve heard great things about the culture here—like the focus on experimentation and giving engineers the space to innovate. That’s the kind of environment where I thrive.

So yeah, I’m really excited about the possibility of contributing here. And I’d love to hear more about what’s next for the team—what are the big challenges or projects on the horizon that someone in this role would help tackle?

Thank you so much for sharing that—it’s been an absolute pleasure getting to know you and hearing about your journey. Your passion for pushing the boundaries of autonomous agents really shines through, and I can tell you’d bring a lot of value to our team. 

As for what’s next, we’re in an exciting phase of scaling our agents to handle more complex, real-world workflows while ensuring they remain reliable and adaptable. There’s a lot of room for innovation, and we’re always looking for people who can bring fresh perspectives and a hands-on approach to problem-solving. 

I’ll make sure to pass along your enthusiasm and insights to the team. You’ll hear from us soon about the next steps, but in the meantime, don’t hesitate to reach out if you have any questions. Thanks again for your time today—it was a genuine pleasure!
